name: Probability Theory
description: Probability measures, random variables, distributions, stochastic processes, martingales, measure-theoretic probability. The mathematics of uncertainty, randomness, and chance — the foundation for all risk assessment, stochastic modeling, Monte Carlo methods, and stochastic calculus (Black-Scholes, Itō calculus). Depends on 444 (algebra via σ-algebras) and 533 (analysis via measure theory and integration). Feeds into 633 (statistics as its inverse problem), 699 (optimization via stochastic optimization), and 700 (numerical methods via Monte Carlo).
axioms: Kolmogorov axioms: non-negativity, unit measure over sample space, countable additivity for disjoint events
Law of Large Numbers (weak & strong): sample mean converges to expectation as n → ∞
Central Limit Theorem: sum of i.i.d. random variables → normal distribution
Borel-Cantelli lemma: infinite occurrences of independent events depend on sum of probabilities
Optional stopping theorem: fair martingales stay fair under bounded stopping times
Itō's lemma: core of stochastic calculus for diffusion processes
reasoning_patterns: Bayesian updating of beliefs from evidence
distribution fitting and selection (Gaussian, Poisson, exponential, heavy-tailed)
expectation, variance, and higher moment computation
tail risk assessment (extreme value theory, VaR, expected shortfall)
stochastic process classification (Markov, Poisson, Wiener, renewal)
Large deviation theory: probability of rare events decays exponentially
boundary_conditions: Probability ≠ frequency for single/unique events (Knightian uncertainty)
Fat-tailed distributions (Cauchy, Pareto with α<2) defy CLT and normal statistics
Prior choice affects posterior — subjectivity is irreducible without strong data
Martingale theory requires careful stopping rules
Measure-theoretic subtleties arise for uncountable sample spaces (non-measurable sets exist under AC)
